title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: The Great Library of Alexandria was once the most significant archive of knowledge in the ancient world, (0) whose destruction remains one of history’s greatest losses. Established during the third century BC, the library aimed to house (9) every single manuscript ever written. Scholars from across the Mediterranean flocked to the city (10) to study its vast and diverse collection of intellectual works. However, (11) despite its prestige, the library’s eventual demise is shrouded in mystery. Some historians argue that it was burned (12) down during Julius Caesar’s siege, (13) while others suggest a gradual decline caused by lack of funding. (14) Not until centuries later did researchers realize that much of the gathered knowledge had actually been preserved elsewhere. (15) Regardless of how it ended, the library’s legacy persists as a symbol of human curiosity and the pursuit of truth. Today, the modern Bibliotheca Alexandrina stands near the original site, serving (16) as a reminder of our shared cultural heritage. It represents an ongoing effort to protect the collective wisdom of humanity against the ravages of time and conflict. By studying its history, we learn that knowledge is fragile and requires constant protection from those who would see it lost.

example.text: The Great Library of Alexandria was once the most significant archive of knowledge in the ancient world, (0) whose destruction remains one of history’s greatest losses.
example.explanation: Possessive relative pronoun referring to 'the Great Library'.

questions.explanation: Determiner used to refer to all members of a group.
Infinitive marker of purpose, indicating why scholars travelled to the city ('flocked to the city to study').
Adverbial particle forming the phrasal verb 'burn down'.
Conjunction used to contrast two different opinions or situations.
Negative adverb required for the emphatic 'Not until' inversion.
Part of the fixed phrase 'Regardless of', meaning 'without being influenced by'.
Preposition used to describe the role or function of something.
